hypothesis: Cross-domain citation rate ~0.1% (1-2/867 lessons via generalizer). ISO annotation provides 20+ cross-domain mapping targets. T4 pass adds 5-10 cross-domain citations raising rate to 0.7-1.2%.
methods: generalizer_expert.py — measures frontier cross-refs (F-XXX spanning multiple domains)
Custom Python analysis of Cites: headers across 867 lessons
Domain extraction from 'Session: SXXX | Domain: XXX' headers
Strict metric: non-meta source and non-meta different-domain target

results.organic_rate_estimate: ~0.1% (unchanged from L-932 S417 after removing tool-added Cites:)

comparison_to_hypothesis.rate_estimate: WRONG: actual frontier-ref rate 5.9% (not 0.1%), Cites-based rate 44.2%
comparison_to_hypothesis.iso_targets: WRONG: not applicable — measurement definition problem identified first
comparison_to_hypothesis.t4_pass: NOT EXECUTED: design flaw discovered — generalizer measures (1) not (3)

key_finding: 3 operational definitions give wildly different answers: frontier cross-refs 5.9%, Cites-based 44.2%, organic ~0.1%. The 44.2% is a tool artifact from lesson_quality_fixer.py. Knowledge silo persists in organic citations. F-EXP8 pre-registered instrument (generalizer) measures metric (1); the F-EXP6 problem was about metric (3). Design flaw: wrong instrument for the hypothesis.
implications: F-EXP8 needs redesign: organic-vs-tool Cites: annotation required before measuring true integration
L-932 measurement was valid at S417 (frontier cross-refs = 0.1%). Now 5.9% = positive signal for frontier-level integration.
lesson_quality_fixer.py created a measurement artifact: organic and tool-added citations now indistinguishable
True test of F-EXP6 open question (close 0.1% gap) requires author-intent tracking
meta_swarm: Target: tools/lesson_quality_fixer.py — should stamp added Cites: with a marker (e.g., '[auto]') to distinguish tool-added from author-written. This restores interpretability of cross-domain metrics.
